data.title: Toro Cut to Market Perform From Outperform by Northland Capital Markets
Sector Update: Consumer
data.description:
Consumer stocks were advancing Friday afternoon, with the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) fractionally higher and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) adding 1.2%.

In corporate news, Manchester United shares jumped 14% after the British soccer club said it expects to hit the top end of its tightened full-year revenue guidance as its fiscal Q3 sales grew year over year.

For further enquiries please contact:

  • Alima Levy

    Elvire Charbonnel

    Olivia Abehassera

  • ir@canal-plus.com

    elvire.charbonnel@canal-plus.com

    olivia.abehassera@canal-plus.com

About CANAL+

Founded as a French subscription-TV channel 40 years ago, CANAL+ is now a global media and entertainment company. The group has 26.9 million subscribers worldwide, over 400 million monthly active users on its OTT and video streaming platforms, and a total of more than 9,000 employees. It generates revenues in 195 countries and operates directly in 52 countries, with leading positions in Pay-TV in 20 of them. CANAL+ operates across the entire audio-visual value chain, including production, broadcast, distribution and aggregation.

It is home to STUDIOCANAL, a leading film and television studio with worldwide production and distribution capabilities; Dailymotion, major international video platform powered by cutting-edge proprietary technology for video delivery, advertising, and monetization; Thema, a production and distribution company specialising in creating and distributing diverse content and channels; and telecommunication services, through GVA in Africa and CANAL+ Telecom in the French overseas jurisdictions and territories. It also operates the iconic performance venues L'Olympia and Théâtre de l'Œuvre in France and CanalOlympia in Africa.

CANAL+ has also significant equity stakes across Africa, Europe and Asia, namely in MultiChoice (the Pay-TV leader in English and Portuguese-speaking Africa), Viaplay (the Pay-TV leader in Scandinavia) and Viu (a leading OTT platform in Southern-Asia).

canalplusgroup.com/en

data.title: Nuclear Vision Looking Raise $2 Million in a Private Placement of Share Units
data.description:
Nuclear Vision (NUKV.CN) on Friday said it is seeking up to $2 million in a non-brokered private placement of share units.

The company will place up to 7.1-million units of the company at a price of $0.28 each and made up of a share and a two-year warrant to buy one share at a price of $0.50.

The company said proceeds will be used to advance its assets in the Republic of Botswana and for general working capital purposes.

The company also said that it has elected not to proceed with the non-brokered private placement announced on January 23.

The company's shares were last seen up $0.01 to $0.32 on the Canadian Securities Exchange.

data.title: Braze Is Maintained at Outperform by Oppenheimer
data.description: (17:18 GMT) Braze Price Target Cut to $44.00/Share From $51.00 by Oppenheimer

data.title: 5N Plus Rating Confirmed and Price Target Raised at National Bank
data.description:
5N Plus had its outperform rating confirmed and its target price raised to $10.50 from $9.00 at National Bank of Canada following positive first quarter results and recent investor meetings.

The bank believes the company stands to benefit from above-contract sales volumes in its renewable energy segment and a sold-out space business.

"It leverages contracts for most of its revenues, and it is vertically integrated into sourcing of rare metals which gives it a strategic position in the supply chain and moats to competition," said analyst Rupert Merer. "It has expertise in chemistry and metallurgy that make it a valuable partner for its customers and its materials are critical, but a relatively low percentage of the end-product cost, giving it pricing power."

National Bank does not believe that VNP should trade at a material discount to its peers, as the company's EBITDA growth, leverage and margins, looks better than most.

data.title: Sector Update: Tech
data.description:
Tech stocks were higher Friday afternoon, with the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) up 0.9% and the SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) climbing 1.8%.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor index added 0.9%.

In corporate news, Apple's outlook is bullish before its developer conference, which is seen as pivotal to its monetization phase of its AI backbone, Wedbush said in a report. Wedbush reiterated its outperform rating. Apple shares were up 1.3%.

data.title: Mexican airport operator ASUR slips as travel hit by US policies
data.description:
** Shares in Mexican airport operator ASUR fall by as much as 2.8% to a one-month low after reporting a decline in May passenger traffic

** ASUR's monthly traffic fell 2% in the month, with the number of international passengers down 3%

** Analysts at Actinver attribute the international drop to "negative sentiment toward immigration policies implemented in the U.S."

** Marco Antonio Montanez at Vector Analisis also cites "a deterioration in U.S. consumer confidence due to President Trump's protectionist trade policy"

** Traffic out of Cancun, a major tourist hub, is slipping as the nearby Tulum airport - run by Mexico's military - ramps up operations, Actinver's Ramon Ortiz adds

** ASUR shares slide as much as 2.8% in mid-morning trading, to their lowest in a month, before paring losses to around 1.7%

** "We aren't ruling out similar behavior in the share price in the short term," Vector adds

data.title: VIRCO MFG CORPORATION SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Virco Mfg Corporation, a leading manufacturer of educational furniture, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of 2025. The report highlights significant financial and operational metrics, reflecting the company's performance amidst a challenging market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Sales: $33.8 million, reflecting a 27.8% decrease compared to the same period last year due to the absence of one-time disaster recovery orders.
  • Gross Profit: $16.0 million, with a gross margin of 47.5% compared to 43.5% in the prior year, attributed to an increase in higher-margin full-service orders.
  • Operating (Loss) Income: $(0.1) million, compared to an operating income of $3.0 million in the prior year, impacted by lower revenue.
  • Net Income: $0.7 million, down from $2.1 million in the same period last year, primarily due to decreased sales.
  • Net Income Per Share - Basic: $0.05, compared to $0.13 in the prior year, reflecting the decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Seasonality Impact: The company's core market for educational furniture is highly seasonal, with approximately 50% of annual revenue recognized in June, July, and August. This seasonality affects production, inventory levels, and accounts receivable management.
  • Revenue Segments: The company experienced a 27.8% decrease in net revenue for the three months ended April 30, 2025, compared to the same period last year, primarily due to the absence of one-time disaster recovery orders that boosted prior year sales.
  • Backlog and Order Management: As of April 30, 2025, the company's backlog was $70.4 million, down from $88.5 million the previous year. Management is moderating production levels to balance on-time summer deliveries with inventory investment.
  • Gross Margin Improvement: The gross margin for the first quarter of 2025 increased to 47.5% from 43.5% in the prior year, attributed to a higher proportion of orders delivered with full service, which carry higher margins.
  • SG&A Expenses: Selling, general, and administrative expenses decreased by approximately $1.3 million due to lower variable selling expenses related to the overall decrease in revenue.
  • Unrealized Gains: The company benefited from $1.2 million in unrealized gains from equity securities in a Rabbi Trust, which partially offset the impact of lower revenue.
  • Interest Expense Reduction: Net interest expense decreased to $60,000 from $208,000 due to reduced demand for seasonal working capital and lower borrowings under the credit facility.
  • Inventory and Material Costs: Inventory levels increased by $2.7 million compared to the previous year, primarily due to increased material costs.
  • Capital Expenditures: Capital expenditures for the three months ended April 30, 2025, were $1.6 million, financed through operating cash flow and restricted by covenant to not exceed $8.0 million per year.
  • Share Repurchase Program: The company repurchased 348,944 shares for $4.0 million during the quarter, with $7.2 million remaining authorized for future repurchases, subject to credit agreement restrictions.
  • Future Outlook: Management anticipates that the majority of the current backlog will be delivered and recognized as revenue during the peak summer months. The company is positioning itself to respond to uncertainties such as trade policies and economic conditions by reinforcing domestic capabilities.

SEC Filing: VIRCO MFG CORPORATION [ VIRC ] - 10-Q - Jun. 06, 2025

data.title: Shaken by crises, Switzerland fetters UBS's global dream
data.description:
Key points:
  • New rules for UBS follow banking crises in 2008 and 2023
  • Higher capital burden expected to weigh on global expansion
  • Proposed measures face long parliamentary process

Switzerland announced reforms on Friday to make its biggest bank UBS safer and avoid another crisis, hampering the global ambitions of a lender whose financial weight eclipses the country's economy.

UBS emerged as Switzerland's sole global bank more than two years ago after the government hastily arranged its rescue of scandal-hit Credit Suisse to prevent a disorderly collapse.

The demise of Credit Suisse, one of the world's biggest banks, rattled global markets and blindsided officials and regulators, whose struggle to steer the lender as it lurched from one scandal to the next underscored their weakness.

On Friday, speaking from the same podium where she had announced the Credit Suisse rescue in 2023 as finance minister, Switzerland's president Karin Keller-Sutter delivered a firm message. The country would not be wrongfooted again.

"I don't believe that the competitiveness will be impaired, but it is true that growth abroad will become more expensive," Keller-Sutter said of UBS.

"We've had two crises. 2008 and 2023," she said. "If you see something that is broken, you have to fix it."

During the global financial crisis of 2008, UBS was hit by a losses in subprime debt, as a disastrous expansion into riskier investment banking forced it to write down tens of billions of dollars and ultimately turn to the state for help.

Memories of that crisis also linger, reinforcing the government's resolve after the collapse of Credit Suisse.

For UBS, which has a financial balance sheet of around $1.7 trillion, far bigger than the Swiss economy, the implications of the reforms proposed on Friday are clear. Switzerland no longer wants to back its international growth.

"Bottom line: who is carrying the risk for growth abroad?" said Keller-Sutter. "The bank, its owners or the state?"

The rules the government proposed demand that UBS in Switzerland holds more capital to cover risks in its foreign operations.

That move, one of the most important steps taken by the Swiss in a series of otherwise piecemeal measures, will make UBS's businesses abroad more expensive to run for one of the globe's largest banks for millionaires and billionaires.

Following publication of the reform plans, UBS Chairman Colm Kelleher and CEO Sergio Ermotti said in an internal memo that if fully implemented, they would undermine the bank's "global competitive footprint" and hurt the Swiss economy.

STRATEGY

The reform would require UBS to hold as much as $26 billion in extra capital.

Some believe the demands may alter the bank's course.

"It could be that UBS has to change its strategy of growth in the United States and Asia," said Andreas Venditti, an analyst at Vontobel.

"It's not just growing. It makes the existing business more expensive. It is an incentive to get smaller and this will most likely happen."

Credit Suisse's demise exploded the myth of invincibility of one of the wealthiest countries in the world, home to a global reserve currency, and proved as unworkable a central reform of the financial crisis to prevent state bailouts.

For many in Switzerland, the government's reforms are long overdue.

"The bank is bigger than the entire Swiss economy. It makes sense that it should not grow even bigger," said Andreas Missbach of Alliance Sud, a group that campaigns for transparency.

"It is good that the government did not give in to lobbying by UBS. The question is whether it is enough. We have a banking crisis roughly every 12 years. So I'm not really put at ease."

UBS CEO Ermotti had lobbied against the reforms, arguing that a heavy capital burden would put the bank on the back foot with rivals.

The world's second-largest wealth manager after Morgan Stanley is dwarfed by its U.S. peer. Morgan Stanley shares value the firm at twice its book value, compared with UBS's 20% premium to book.

On Friday, the bank reiterated this message, saying that it strongly disagreed with the "extreme" increase in capital.

But others are sceptical that the government has done enough.

Hans Gersbach, a professor at ETH Zurich, said there was still no proper plan to cope should UBS run into trouble.

"The credibility of the too big to fail regime remains in question."

data.title: Slashing Credits Would Give Tesla's Margins a Haircut
data.description:
By Dan Gallagher

Tesla can survive the loss of tax credits. But they risk making the EV pioneer look more like any other automaker-when the market values it as anything but.

President Trump has threatened to eliminate "Elon's Governmental Subsidies and Contracts" in his highly public feud with Tesla Chief Executive Elon Musk. That after the "big, beautiful" tax bill that passed a narrow House vote last month included provisions to largely phase out tax credits for electric vehicles and other clean energy programs.

Tesla also benefits from regulatory credits, which it sells to other automakers to help them meet emissions requirements. That makes it vulnerable to any attempt by Republicans to loosen those requirements.

Eliminating tax credits for consumers would hit demand. The starting price of Tesla's two most popular cars-the Model Y and Model 3-would be about 17% more expensive without the current $7,500 federal credit, according to pricing details on Tesla's website.

Regulatory credits, on the other hand, contribute directly to Tesla's revenue. It reported about $2.76 billion in automotive regulatory credits for 2024, or about 4% of its total automotive revenue for the year. But that small revenue stream is very profitable; Tesla says such credits "have negligible incremental costs associated with them" in its annual filings. Stripping out the credits would lower Tesla's gross profit margin by three percentage points, to 15.4% for the year.

That would actually come in a bit below the 16.2% annual gross margin averaged by the 10 largest automobile manufacturers by annual revenue, according to data from S&P Global Market Intelligence.

But Tesla's market capitalization of just under $1 trillion still exceeds the combined value of the 10 larger auto manufacturers. That's because Tesla is mostly valued on what it hasn't done yet, like launching a large-scale robotaxi service or populating the nation's homes with its Optimus robot. Still, a loss of government support would hit the company hard.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: SOC ALERT: Levi & Korsinsky Investigates Sable Offshore Corp. for Possible Securities Fraud Violations
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / June 6, 2025 / Levi & Korsinsky notifies investors that it has commenced an investigation of Sable Offshore Corp. ("Sable Offshore Corp.") concerning possible violations of federal securities laws.

Sable announced on May 19, 2025, that it had resumed oil production from one of three offshore platforms related to its Las Flores pipeline (the "Onshore Pipeline") in California as of May 15, 2025. The California State Land Commission sent Sable a letter warning the Company that, "The [May 19] press release appears to mischaracterize the nature of recent activities, causing significant public confusion and raising questions regarding Sable's intentions." According to the letter, Sable had conflated offshore well testing activities required by a federal regulatory agency with the restart of operations.

On May 28, 2025, the Santa Barbara County Superior Court approved a preliminary injunction requested by the California Coastal Commission regarding Sable's maintenance and repair work in the coastal zone related to its Onshore Pipeline.

Following this news, the price of Sable fell over 15% on May 28, 2025. To obtain additional information, go to:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/sable-offshore-corp-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=152011&wire=1&utm_campaign=42

data.title: Omada Health Shares Jump After IPO
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Omada Health shares climbed after the company made its debut on Nasdaq.

The virtual healthcare company started trading shares at $19 on Friday morning, and they jumped 35% to $25.58 by midday. It touched a high of $28.40.

Omada was founded in 2011 and offers virtual services to people with chronic diseases, primarily those that are related to obesity such as diabetes and hypertension.

The company expects the rise in GLP-1s such as Wegovy and Zepbound will help drive the business. It offers complementary services to help people on the weight-loss drugs to lose and keep off weight.

Omada previously said it expects to have about 56.9 million shares outstanding after the IPO, making the market capitalization $1.08 billion with the $19 opening price.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: LASE ALERT: Levi & Korsinsky Investigates Laser Photonics Corporation for Possible Securities Fraud Violations
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / June 6, 2025 / Levi & Korsinsky notifies investors that it has commenced an investigation of Laser Photonics Corporation ("Laser Photonics Corporation") concerning possible violations of federal securities laws.

Laser Photonics disclosed in a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on May 29, 2025, that it had "received a notice from Nasdaq Listing Qualifications department of the Nasdaq Stock Market LLC (‘Nasdaq') stating that since it had not received the Company's Form 10-Q for the period ended March 31, 2025, and because the Company remains delinquent in filing its Form 10-K for the period ended December 31, 2024 (the ‘Initial Delinquent Filing'), the Company does not comply with Nasdaq's Listing Rules for continued listing." Laser Photonics said that "the Company has until June 16, 2025, to submit a plan to regain compliance with respect to these delinquent reports." Laser Photonics specified that the delay was "primarily due to [Laser Photonics'] acquisition of certain assets of CMS in November 2024 . . . , approximately six weeks before the end of [Laser Photonics'] fiscal year, for approximately $1 million out of the bankruptcy proceedings of CMS's former parent company."

Following this news, Laser Photonics' stock price fell over 6% on May 30, 2025. To obtain additional information, go to:

data.title: FuelCell Energy Rises on New Layoff Round, Further Restructuring Efforts
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Shares of FuelCell Energy jumped after energy platform solutions company said it would lay off more staff as part of its restructuring efforts.

The stock rose 34%, to $16.73, midday Friday. It has fallen about 22% so far this year.

The Danbury, Conn., company plans to cut another 22% of its staff from global operations in the U.S., Canada and Germany as part of its efforts to scale core carbonate technologies and achieve profitability.

In November, FuelCell began its first cuts, which included trimming 17% of its workforce to save about 15% on operating costs.

After the cuts, the company said it would have 426 global employees remaining.

Other elements of the restructuring plan include a reduction in discretionary overhead spending, adjustments to its manufacturing schedule and deferral of compensation and benefits.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 1 PM ET: Hiring Slowed in May, With 139,000 New Jobs | Jobs ...
data.description:
Hiring Slowed in May, With 139,000 New Jobs

The U.S. added 139,000 jobs in May, and the unemployment rate held steady at 4.2%.

----

Jobs Report Lifts Stocks as Trump Calls on Fed to Slash Rates

U.S. stocks rose after the jobs report showed hiring slowed in May, but by less than expected.

----

ECB Has Won Battle Against Inflation in the Eurozone, Villeroy Says

Europe has succeeded in bringing inflation under control after a surge driven by the end of the Covid-19 pandemic and Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the head of France's central bank said.

----

European Industry Contracts as Tariffs Pull Exports Lower

Industrial production in France and Germany declined in the first month of Trump's global tariff blitz, with goods exports to the U.S. from the latter also falling sharply.

----

The Future Ain't What It Used to Be for These Funds

Wall Street wants to sell more 'alternative' assets to small investors. The glory days for these assets may already be ending.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Inflation Data Could Show Tariffs Feeding Into Higher Prices

Further comments from U.S. President Trump on tariffs, as well as developments with Trump's feud with Elon Musk will continue to be closely monitored.

----

India Central Bank Delivers Larger-Than-Expected Rate Cut to Support Growth

India's central bank surprised markets with a jumbo rate cut, seizing on cooling inflation to frontload monetary easing and bolster economic growth amid heightened global uncertainty.

----

China Central Bank Pumps Liquidity Into Markets

The move is likely aimed at cushioning against an emerging cash crunch as trade tensions simmer.

----

Russia Cuts Key Rate Despite High Inflation

The Bank of Russia lowered its key interest rate for the first time since late 2022, even with inflation remaining well above its target.

----

RBA Upbeat on China's Willingness to Do What It Takes on Growth

High-profile visits by senior Australian central bank officials have returned a verdict of confidence that China remains well-placed to ride out the upheavals emerging from U.S. trade policy.

----

Wall Street Is Too Pessimistic on the Dollar. That Could Be a Problem.

The dollar is struggling, and strategists overwhelmingly agree it's heading even lower. But with such strong consensus on a negative outlook, any positive news on the greenback could deliver an unexpected-and much harder-blow.

----

Congress Will Open Private Markets to Everyone, Next Year

By the end of 2026, Congress will relax the qualifications for investing in private equity and credit, TD Cowen analysts say.

----

Oil Prices Have Stayed Unexpectedly Strong. One Wildcard Could Keep Them Rising.

----

Treasury to Double Down on Currency Manipulation, Issues Warning for China

Recent weakness in the dollar gives trading partners more reason to intervene, pushing down their own currencies' value.

----

Apple's Growing List of Problems Clouds AI Reboot

What Musk and Trump Risk Losing in Their High-Stakes Breakup

Despite Trade War, U.S. Drug Companies Turn to China for Key Cancer Treatments

Here Come the Berkshire Hathaway Wannabes

These companies are seeking to replicate Warren Buffett's formula as he prepares to retire. Some have been big winners.

The annual report disclosed that on February 11, 2025, Semler Scientific “began initial settlement discussions with DOJ [(the United States Department of Justice)], but ceased initial discussions on that date. Accordingly, there is a risk that DOJ will file a complaint or complaint in intervention in a civil False Claims Act lawsuit seeking damages. [Semler Scientific] does not believe the amount of loss can be reasonably estimated.”

On this news, Semler Scientific’s stock fell over 9% on the next trading day.

Rosen Law Firm achieved the largest ever securities class action settlement against a Chinese Company at the time. At the time Rosen Law Firm was Ranked No. 1 by ISS Securities Class Action Services for number of securities class action settlements in 2017. The firm has been ranked in the top 4 each year since 2013 and has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors. In 2019 alone the firm secured over $438 million for investors. data.title: Porsche not planning to shift final assembly of cars to US
data.description:
Porsche has no plans to shift any part of its production process to the U.S., a spokesperson said on Friday, dismissing a Bloomberg report saying the company was considering such a move to mitigate the effects of tariffs.

The luxury carmaker, like Volkswagen's Audi , does not produce vehicles in the U.S., leaving it heavily exposed to tariffs at a time when it faces the challenges of falling demand, fierce competition in China and slow uptake of its electric models.

Bloomberg, citing unnamed sources, reported on Friday that Porsche CEO Oliver Blume, who also leads the wider Volkswagen Group, was considering moving the final stages of model assembly, such as the installation of interior components or fitting of tires to the United States.

A spokesperson for the carmaker said no such plans were in place.

Porsche's finance chief Jochen Breckner said in late April that localisation made no sense given its low vehicle sales, even if the group were to team up with another VW brand.

Volkswagen's Audi brand plans to produce its best-selling models in the U.S. and will announce a location this year, although the brand has said that the plan pre-dates the Trump administration.

Blume said last week that he had engaged in discussions with Washington to expand the Volkswagen Group's presence in the United States through "massive investments", but declined to provide details, citing confidentiality.

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Edges Up to 7% in May — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--The trade war with the U.S. is taking its toll on Canada's labor market, though pockets of resilience point to softness rather than a collapse, even if finding work is becoming tougher.

Employers in the country added a modest 8,800 jobs in May, Statistics Canada said Friday. That was better than the 12,500 jobs economists expected would be lost, but it roughly matches the weak pace seen the month before and extends what has effectively been a stretch of no employment growth since January's jump.

And while population growth has slowed sharply to its slowest level in four years, the expansion in Canada's labor force for the month and lackluster hiring meant the unemployment rate ticked up to 7.0% last month--the highest rate since September 2016 outside of the peak pandemic years.

Encouragingly, the latest labor-market survey showed total private-sector employment rebounded for the month, rising by 60,600, and growth in full-time positions was strong. Yet industries directly exposed to the import tariffs imposed by the Trump administration have been shedding jobs. And employment in May was dragged down by the loss of 32,000 public-administration workers hired temporarily to work during April's federal election.

"The May jobs report definitely could have been worse... any increase is a small win," Douglas Porter, chief economist at Bank of Montreal, said. "But, standing back, the bigger picture is that the manufacturing sector is under intense strain amid the deep trade uncertainty, and the overall job market continues to soften, highlighted by the grinding rise in the unemployment rate."

Porter said the mixed report for May doesn't give a clear signal to the Bank of Canada, which this week opted to leave its policy interest rate steady, though the rising jobless rate is likely to keep officials in easing mode through the second half of the year.

Uncertainty prevails for business and households in Canada, and surveys indicate companies plan to scale back investment and have dropped hiring plans. The central bank is watching for evidence the economy is buckling under the stresses of higher tariffs and reduced demand. But in keeping rates steady this week, it noted there had been softness but not a sharp weakening in the early months of 2025. It has signaled the door is open to resuming rate cuts, provided the new levies don't stoke inflation.

"Most businesses expect activity to weaken in the near term, which puts jobs at risk," Deputy Gov. Sharon Kozicki said in a speech Thursday.

Total private-sector employment rebounded in May, with 60,600 jobs added, recouping most of the losses from the prior two months. Employment growth was noted in services positions, with a collective rise of about 43,000 seen in wholesale and retail trade at a time when "Buy Canadian" campaigns are being promoted as a response to U.S. trade policies. Growth was also logged in culture and recreation jobs, as well as in areas including finance and real estate and utilities.

Manufacturing employment, however, fell for a fourth straight month, and that area has now lost roughly 55,000 jobs in that time. There also was a 15,500-job drop in transportation and warehousing employment in May, a further sign that businesses' efforts to rapidly build inventories to beat the new tariffs have ended.

The threats and imposition of U.S. tariffs targeting the automotive industry also look to have taken a toll. The highest unemployment rates among Canada's largest cities were in southern Ontario in Windsor, Oshawa and Toronto, all hubs for auto manufacturing. General Motors in early May said it was eliminating a shift at a southern-Ontario pickup-truck plant to reflect the trade environment.

The weakness in manufacturing may also explain why total hours worked were unchanged in May from a month prior, though they were up 0.9% compared with a year earlier.

The risk of more widespread layoffs and weak hiring intentions indicate the unemployment rate in Canada could climb further in the months ahead, though economist views vary on just how high the rate will rise and on how the central bank will respond.

Claire Fan, senior economist at Royal Bank of Canada, expects that trade disruptions will remain a headwind but that the deterioration in the job market will be contained, and she anticipates the unemployment rate will peak only slightly above May's 7.0% reading. Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport said he thinks a recession is already underway that it will soon broaden to other sectors and drive the jobless rate to about 7.5% by year-end.

As of May, there were about 1.6 million Canadians out of work. There are signs that finding work has gotten tougher and that the summer job market for youth will be weak.

A smaller number of Canadians who were unemployed in April found work the following month compared with a year earlier, as well as compared with the pre-pandemic average for May. The average duration of unemployment has risen as well, and people without a job are spending an average of 21.8 weeks searching for work in May compared with 18.4 weeks the same month last year.

May marks the beginning of the student summer-job season, but it was off to a weak start, as the jobless rate among returning students aged 15 to 24 was up 3.2 percentage points on a year earlier at 20.1%. That's in keeping with data compiled by the research arm of online job-site Indeed, which showed summer job postings as of early May were down 22% on a year earlier, with declines seen in ads for summer-camp roles, painters, lifeguards and other positions.
